methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $100M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 1 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Quantum Machines is a Series B-stage quantum computing company that has raised $100M+ to date. The quantum computing peer universe comprises 11 total comparables: 3 public companies (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum) and 8 private peers including Q-CTRL, Quantinuum, and others.

Valuation analysis relies on a single applicable method due to peer set constraints. Stage-progression methodology, anchoring to the $100M total raised as a proxy baseline, applies a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical for Series B progression. This yields an implied valuation of $190M. A peer-median approach was not viable; while 7 same-sector comparables exist, filtering for same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-tier companies produced fewer than three datapoints, making narrow peer comparison statistically unreliable. Broad cross-sector median benchmarking would introduce excessive noise.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $162M to $218M, with a median of $190M. This range reflects ±15% sensitivity around the stage-progression result. Key uncertainties include the absence of a robust same-stage peer cohort for validation, reliance on a raised-capital proxy rather than disclosed post-money valuations, and the volatility in quantum computing venture financing. Additional transparency on Quantum Machines' specific technological differentiation, customer traction, or burn rate would refine this estimate.
